item.title: Interview: Talking Magic: The Gathering's The Hobbit Book-First Approach To Building Its Middle-earth Follow-Up
item.content:encoded:
I don’t think it’s any secret that I am a massive fan of Magic: The Gathering’s The Lord of the Rings: Tales of Middle-earth set that released a few years back. It’s the Magic set of my dreams, and it brought so many of my favorite characters, moments, and stories to life in a new and interesting way. I’ve always wondered how the Wizards of the Coast team would follow this set up. 

Tales of Middle-earth covered the gamut of storytelling found within the pages of The Lord of the Rings trilogy, from the finding of the Ring to its destruction and everything in between. It was a set that proved the Wizards team could blend storytelling from another universe with the mechanics that make Magic: The Gathering such a compelling card game decades after its release, and while we have seen future Universes Beyond sets attempt this, only a few have been as successful. 

So when the Wizards team gave us our first glimpse of The Hobbit during MagicCon Vegas’ Preview Panel, I got pretty excited, since it looks like this set, even from this initial glimpse, is going to continue on with the foundation built by The Lord of the Rings: Tales of Middle-earth.

During the Preview Panel, we saw some of the first cards for the upcoming Universes Beyond set releasing this Summer, including an iconic scene that has ramifications for Tolkien’s later work, Riddles in the Dark. 

For those who have never read The Hobbit, or gotten deep enough in its publication weeds as I have, depending on which version of The Hobbit you’re reading, you might see a completely different Riddles in the Dark chapter. This difference is something Tolkien himself gives a nod to in The Council of Elrond in The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring when Bilbo recounts his tale to the Council.

When asked by Elrond to recount the finding of the Ring, Bilbo apologizes to those who might have heard a different version, because indeed the retelling in the original version of The Hobbit was a bit different than what most of us read growing up. 

“Very well,’ said Bilbo. ‘I will do as you bid. But I will now tell the true story, and if some here have heard me tell it otherwise’ – he looked sidelong at Glóin – ‘I ask them to forget it and forgive me. I only wished to claim the treasure as my very own in those days, and to be rid of the name of thief that was put on me. But perhaps I understand things a little better now. Anyway, this is what happened."

Tolkien, in the Second Edition of The Hobbit, effectively retcons himself by subtly shifting the mood in the Riddles in the Dark scene, making Gollum appear darker, more sinister, more scary. I actually wondered whether the team would have any interesting interplay between these two depictions of the character on the cards, or whether they would stick completely with the most current version of The Hobbit. 

“It’s the most well-known,” Nick Marcantel, Creative Set Lead on The Hobbit set told me during an interview at MagicCon Vegas. “It’s the one that came after Lord of the Rings, it’s the one that Tolkien went back to rewrite and get some of those details to better connect what he wrote for The Lord of the Rings.”

Mike Turian, a senior executive producer on Magic: The Gathering, added a more practical reason for sticking to just one edition of The Hobbit: translations.

Magic isn’t printed in just English, and as a result, the consideration had to be made for everything that is printed on the card, including the flavor text - especially flavor text that is printing genuine Tolkien lines. 

“So there’s the English version, right, but then also there is the conversation around, the book, the hobby gets localized,” Turian explained. He continued, “And so then we also had to work with Middle-earth Enterprises of, ‘Hey, do we take from the editions that we used and do our own localization?’ Which I believe is the path we went down, we also did that for The Lord of the Rings: Tales of Middle-earth. Or it could have been, ‘Let’s look at the French edition, let’s look at the German edition.’ And pulled quotes from that. So that’s another place where we had to navigate editions. And I'm glad we did it the way we did, where even though it may not be word-for-word identical due to just localization differences, we’re making sure we’re capturing the same spirit and same meaning overall.”

Staying true to the text of the book was important to The Hobbit dev team overall, including the book’s tone, especially both its whimsical parts and the darker themes that are woven in there, especially the reworking of Riddles in the Dark. 

The Hobbit is, overall, a very different read than Tolkien’s Lord of the Rings or the more Ecclesiastical styling of The Silmarillion. The Hobbit is, first and foremost, a children’s story, and its narrative path and storytelling, which touch on some dark themes, are broadly more upbeat, whimsical, and just plain different than the narration we get in either of Tolkien’s other big works. 

Nick mentions that their “utmost goal” on the team was to make sure the story they were representing on the cards hit those tones first.

“It was our utmost goal to hit the tones of the novel first: it being a whimsical children’s fantasy story was really important to us. We know going into it from reading and working on [The] Lord of the Rings that this was just going to always feel different. Tolkien uses a different tone, right? A different voice here in this novel, and that was going to carry, we did feel very strongly about capturing that essence here in this set.

“The darker elements that make up this world, we did want this to link and be cohesive with Tales of Middle-earth, so we did not shy away from those elements. Like, we really wanted to capture Riddles in the Dark, the destruction of Lake Town, the Battle of Five Armies - all this stuff was important.”

Principal game designer and set lead on The Hobbit, Annie Sardelis, added some insight into the game design aspects of how the team approached The Hobbit’s set, especially since this is technically a “return” set in Magic’s pantheon of set releases, but as Annie puts it, it’s "quite different” from doing a standard return set. 

“It is quite different than doing a ‘return to,’ for example. It’s like with the original Lorwyn, and then we got Lorwyn Eclipsed, it’s like, ‘Oh, well, what mechanics are we going to see returning? What characters are we going to see returning?’ I’ve seen people asking, like, ‘I wonder what the new Legolas is going to do? I wonder if we’re going to have The Ring Tempts you?’ And I’m like, those aren’t really in the book. So first and foremost, we do have to revisit it from just the story of The Hobbit. But what could be a natural add-on, or a reference people might like?”

This is where the team looked at Box Toppers as a way to bring back cards and mechanics from the previous Middle-earth set, but not introduce them into the Hobbit main set - cards like the iconic The One Ring, or reprints of powerful characters such as Sauron. Sardelis mentions the goal was to find a way to include these references to the broader world of Middle-earth without the set feeling “dissonant.”

Sardelis brings up Sauron, who technically appears in The Hobbit but not in the form we know him in the broader Lord of the Rings volumes. Introducing characters and themes that we know, in hindsight, were there all along might make some sense, but the team wanted to stay as true to the book as possible, meaning we won’t be seeing Sauron take on Thorin Oakenshield in an upcoming draft night. 

Don’t rule out the Necromancer, though. 

This filling in the gaps does seem to be happening in other places, though, such as Turian’s description of Dwarven women who, we all know, must have existed, but aren’t featured in the books. Indeed, the team is going far enough to give The Elven-king his actual name, Thranduil, despite that name appearing for the first time in The Fellowship of the Ring and not in The Hobbit proper. 

I like this approach, honestly, because it fleshes out the world of The Hobbit, which, when taken in a vacuum, isn’t as broad as the world explored in The Lord of the Rings, but it does so in a way that stays true to the book itself. 

Expanding too much for the sake of expansion was honestly one of my biggest issues with The Hobbit movies. It wasn’t that there were additions or characters added to the story that weren’t there in the novel. It was that it was so dramatically different than Tolkien’s original work that it no longer represented or felt like what Tolkien had carefully written. The approach the MTG Hobbit team is taking here is sticking as true to the novel as possible and building around the edges where it feels appropriate (and building based on what we know of the book, not simply adding their own lore, which is crucial).

Mechanically, this also allows some room to explore the growth and changes characters in The Hobbit undergo during the adventure. We see this in the versions of Bilbo the team showcased during the Preview Panel over the weekend. 

Bilbo, Luckwearer is a 1/1 creature that can’t be blocked (thanks to The Ring), and when he deals combat damage, you loot. Burglar’s Plot, the Adventure side Bilbo gets to go on, lets you exchange control over two different nonland permanents that share a type - basically Bilbo swiping something of yours and replacing it with something similar. I’m getting vibes of Dr. Jones taking treasure and replacing it with a bag of the same approximate weight here.

Meanwhile, Bilbo’s Mythic Rare version, Bilbo, Thief in the Night, sees a fully realized burglar, giving you a cost reduction to spells cast anywhere other than your hand, and allowing you cast an Artifact, instant or sorcery card from your graveyard when he attacks. Bilbo himself is a smidge stronger as well, a 2/2 this time around. 

I’m eager to see if there is another Bilbo coming down the pipeline here that better represents the Hobbit as he starts his adventure, or as he wrestles between his Baggins and Took sides for dominance. 

Other card designs feel inspired, such as the iconic Trolls, Tom, Bert, and William. A powerful group of creatures in their own right, the three monstrous trolls act as a sacrifice outlet, enabling card draw while giving you a powerful 5/5 creature in the process. And, just like Trolls when they get turned to stone in The Hobbit, these Trolls don’t go anywhere when they die - instead, they return to the battlefield as an artifact. Great stuff.

Smaug’s characterization might be the most intriguing to me, though. While we all know of the great dragon for his calamitous side, he’s also vain, cunning, and deeply intelligent in a way that rivals his greed and lust for more wealth. So far we only know of Smaug, the Magnificent, a flying/haste dragon that cares about treasure tokens. I am interested to see what other designs The Hobbit team has in store for the great worm, especially as they depict his iconic scene with the foremost Hobbit burglar from Bag End.

Capturing a character’s whole story isn’t simply relegated to character-specific cards, though. 

“All of this is to say the progression of the characters was really important to us,” Nick says. He continued, “You’re going to see characters who have their own cards and their own colors, but we wanted to show the entire story, so you are going to see other spells where characters are captured in different iterations and expressions throughout the set.”

“You literally have it chapter by chapter,” Annie added with a chuckle. 

The decision to include The One Ring only as a box topper, rather than in the main set, was also something we discussed, as I think many onlookers would expect the powerful Ring of Power to make an appearance. 

However, from a literary standpoint, the Ring as we know it in The Lord of the Rings, wasn’t necessarily depicted as this great evil in The Hobbit. Instead, the Ring is much more understated. It’s an important part of the story for sure - Bilbo’s career trajectory as a burglar is dependent on his finding of the Ring of Power - but it’s not the cornerstone the story rests upon.

But, it is important enough to be there, so the team came up with depicting the Ring from Gollum’s perspective: My Precious. 

For Annie, it seems the answer to approach it this way was simple. 

“As a designer, I just want to design new stuff. Let me take my own stab at it.”

My Precious is a 3-cost artifact equipment that gives the equipped creature hexproof and makes it unblockable. However, its equip cost does reflect the Ring’s influence - it costs not just mana, but life to put on the Ring. 

It also has an adventure side, Allure of Power, which is a sacrifice outlet but draws you two cards for your trouble. 

Annie mentions as well that there is some cool interplay between My Precious and Bilbo’s adventure side, meaning that as Bilbo, you could literally steal the Ring using Burglar’s Plot. 

I mean, as a Tolkien fan, that kind of synergistic storytelling is so cool. Because, in effect, in a game of Commander, you could swap a copy of the full-fat version of The One Ring with an Arcane Signet, for example. I love this.

At the end of the day, I’m incredibly excited to see where else The Hobbit takes us. The Magic: The Gathering team’s first attempt at Tolkien’s universe worked out extremely well, and I’m excited to see the full set when it starts previewing ahead of its August release date. 

Joseph Bradford

Joseph has been writing or podcasting about games in some form since about 2012. Having written for multiple major outlets such as IGN, Playboy, and more, Joseph started writing for MMORPG in 2015. When he's not writing or talking about games, you can typically find him hanging out with his 15-year old or playing Magic: The Gathering with his family. Also, don't get him started on why Balrogs *don't* have wings. You can find him on Twitter @LotrLore



https://www.mmorpg.com/interviews/interview-talking-magic-the-gatherings-the-hobbit-book-first-approach-to-building-its-middle-earth-follow-up-2000137994

item.title: EVE Online’s Studio Walks Away From Pearl Abyss After Record $70M Year, Reborn as Fenris Creations With Google DeepMind Backing
item.content:encoded:
Following the sale of CCP Games from Pearl Abyss for $120 million in cash and non-cash elements, the Icelandic studio known mainly for the MMORPG EVE Online announced that it is rebranding and will be called Fenris Creations henceforth. The split was the result of a joint strategic review between the two parties, who mutually concluded that independent ownership was the better fit, citing "broader differences in operating context, current strategic focus, and long-term priorities".

The new ownership group comprises Fenris Creations' senior management and long-term investors, including Omega Ventures co-founder Birgir Már Ragnarsson, who takes over as Board Chairman. CEO Hilmar Veigar Pétursson confirmed there will be no layoffs, office closures, or restructuring as part of the transition; he also underlined that EVE Online delivered record revenue in November 2025, its second-highest revenue quarter in history in Q4, and closed the year with over $70 million in reported revenue, some of its best financial results in years.

Alongside the independence announcement, today Fenris Creations revealed a new research partnership with Google DeepMind, with Google also taking a minority equity stake in the company. The research focus covers:

  • Long-horizon planning
  • Memory and continual learning
  • Intelligence in complex, dynamic, player-driven systems

DeepMind will work with an offline version of EVE Online running on a local server to test and evaluate AI models in a controlled setting, and the partnership will also explore new gameplay experiences enabled by these technologies. Google DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis explicitly tied his interest to his own gaming background and to the lab's history of game-based AI research (Atari DQN, AlphaGo, AlphaStar, SIMA), calling EVE Online "amazingly complex" as a research environment.

As a reminder, CCP Games (now Fenris Creations) is currently developing the extraction shooter game EVE Vanguard, which is connected to EVE Online, and the survival spin-off EVE Frontier, in addition to maintaining the long-running EVE Online.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/eve-online-fenris-creations-pearl-abyss-google-deepmind/

item.title: Unleash Your Inner Goblin With The New Secret Lair Commander Deck: Goblin Storm
item.content:encoded:
Wizards of the Coast has announced a new striking, and pricey Secret Lair Commander Deck. Secret Lair Commander Deck: Goblin Storm is all about goblins causing chaos, all while sporting artwork by Wizard of Barge.

According to an official press release, Secret Lair Commander Deck: Goblin Storm was designed by Carmen Klomparens and Eli Rice. The deck is a mix of classic, fast-paced goblin attacks and the exercise of patience regarding the deck's unique Storm mechanic. 

The 100-card deck will sport 12 borderless foil cards. These foils include Commander Zada, Hedron Grinder, Krenko, Mob Boss, Broadside Bombadiers, Grapeshot, Roaming Throne, and more. These foils, alongside 22 Mountain land cards, will all sport Wizard of Barge's artwork, depicting the goblins as adorable, impish little scamps. This is expected given the artist's past work on Magic: The Gathering.

Lastly, the presser confirms that Secret Lair Commander Deck: Goblin Storm will come in a special deck box with additional products. This includes a storage box, foil Goblin token cards, and a Storm Count tracker, also featuring Wizard of Barge's artwork.

“Secret Lair has been long known for its love of meteorological phenomena, and to answer the timeless philosophical question, ‘How many Goblins can I accumulate after I storm off to bash my opponents’ face in?’” said Secret Lair Principal Product Lead Steve Sunu. “If you understood any of that last sentence, this is the deck for you! We tasked Wizards of the Coast’s best designers and asked one of Secret Lair’s fan-favorite artists—and longtime Goblin enjoyer—to help us help you develop your own answer to that question.”

Secret Lair Commander Deck: Goblin Storm will be available for purchase on the Secret Lair website on May 18th, 2026, at 9:00 AM Pacific time. It will be sold at an MSRP of $149.99 and will be available while supplies last.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/unleash-your-inner-goblin-with-new-secret-lair-commander-deck-goblin-storm

item.title: Mina The Hollower Has a New Release Date And An Aggressively Low Price
item.content:encoded:
Mina the Hollower was supposed to be out last October, but it got delayed at the last minute as Shovel Knight maker Yacht Club Games pulled out all the stops to try to make its upcoming retro mash-up of Castlevania and Link’s Awakening as good a game as possible. It’s now back with a new release date that’s just around the corner and I’m looking forward to it more than ever.

The new day of reckoning is May 29. Mina the Hollower will bring its top-down dungeon combat and puzzles to consoles and PC for just $20. That’s the unofficial new price ceiling for 2D indie action games as set last fall by the launch of Hollow Knight: Silksong, a GOTY contender fans couldn’t believe was as cheap as it was. Some other recent gems have struggled when going above that threshold like March’s Scott Pilgrim EX, an excellent River City Ransom-style beat ’em up that has stalled on Steam at $29.

I wouldn’t normally engage in pre-launch armchair analysis of the business dynamics facing a new indie game but Yacht Club is a great studio and Mina the Hollower is a game I really want to see succeed.

TRUE FINAL RELEASE DATE!! 🐭🙌
Mina the Hollower launches May 29 for only $19.99!
Prepare yourself for one of the greatest top-down adventures ever to be delivered directly to your soul for less than 20 bucks.
Mark your calendars. Tell your friends. Wishlist now – links below!🧵 pic.twitter.com/exCI1cFwSg

— Yacht Club Games (@YachtClubGames) May 6, 2026

“It’s make-or-break for sure,” studio founder Sean Velasco told Bloomberg about the game’s launch last December. “If we sold 500,000 copies, then we would be golden. If we sold even 200,000, that would be really great. If we sold, like, 100,000, that’s not so good.”

When I demoed the game at PAX West months before that in September, the game seemed in a good place, but these days anything short of exceptional can be a big dice roll. Even great games don’t always break through anymore. Shovel Knight was one of a kind back when it came out in 2014. The bar for standing out in 2026 has risen exponentially.

Fortunately, it feels like Mina the Hollower is coming out at just the right moment to capitalize on the post-spring lull and the peak summer nostalgia of a retro adventure that would look right at home on the Game Boy Advance.



https://kotaku.com/mina-the-hollower-has-a-new-release-date-and-an-aggressively-low-price-2000693637

item.title: 007 First Light Will Not Offer New Game+
item.content:encoded:
It was recently confirmed during a developer FAQ stream that 007 First Light will not offer New Game+ at launch, and there are no plans to introduce it in the future. Instead, there will be a focus on replayability through TacSim, a mode that allows missions to be replayed with added modifiers and challenges.

This follows the news that a sequel could be on the cards if the game proves to be popular enough, as well as the fact that the Nintendo Switch 2 release could be delayed again.

Additional 007 First Light Developer Details

The lack of New Game+ was just a drop in the water during the developer stream, where numerous frequently asked questions were answered. Not only did they explain the status of multiplayer and a demo, but it was revealed that an online connection will not be required after the game’s initial download.

As for gameplay details, there will be a “constant flow” of new stuff to unlock, which ranges from new gadgets and gizmos to suits, though these are mainly limited to TacSim to maintain the campaign’s tone. They’re earned by completing challenges, which encourage you to complete the missions in multiple ways.

That said, there isn’t any progression in the traditional sense, with Bond not able to unlock new skills; instead, the gadgets will serve as your progression. At the start of each mission, you’ll have to decide which ones you want to bring with you, and you can even use them quickly during combat.

If you do break out into a scuffle, which is expected to happen quite a lot, then you can use a lot more than your own gear. You’ll have to mix between the combo-based melee system and the various weapons you can find during stages to get the upper hand against your enemies—or you can always bluff them, and avoid a fight altogether.

007 First Light is set to launch on May 27, so you don’t have to wait long until you can unpack all of this for yourself.

Are you excited for 007 First Light? Share your thoughts in the official Insider Gaming Discord Server.

For more 007 First Light coverage, check out how long the campaign should last, as well as the full voice cast.

Charlie is one of Insider Gaming's Guides Writers who's been in the gaming industry for a little over a year. Unfortunately for him, he enjoys every video game genre out…

item.title: Ed Boon Would Love to See a Street Fighter Character in Mortal Kombat, Plays Down Rivalry
item.content:encoded:
A showdown for the ages.

Mortal Kombat co-creator Ed Boon has revealed his wishlist for future guest characters in the fighting game series.

In a new interview with Collider while promoting the Mortal Kombat 2 movie, Boon confirmed another Mortal Kombat game is being planned at NetherRealm, but failed to share details. However, when asked which guest characters he wanted to see in the future, he mentioned Street Fighter, which, if it were to actually happen, would finally provide fighting game fans with the crossover they've been waiting 30 years for.

“I would love to see a Street Fighter character in Mortal Kombat. I’m a huge fan of Street Fighter,” Boon said. "We’ve had Joker as a really dark character, but DC and Marvel, it would be a lot of fun to see those characters as well.”

Mortal Kombat Flawless Victory: A Visual History of the Iconic Series Excerpt

Mortal Kombat is famous for its guest characters, which typically skew on the more violent side of entertainment. Fans have already seen the likes of The Terminator, Ghostface, and Rambo guest star in Mortal Kombat. Perhaps, if Marvel were to give the green light, Deadpool and Wolverine would be a good fit.

But is it likely that any of this would actually happen? A Mortal Kombat vs Street Fighter game has been talked about for years, but there's no indication Capcom would ever go for it. Street Fighter is nowhere near as gory as Mortal Kombat, which, if it were to have the likes of Ryu and Chun-Li included, would have to see them ripped to shreds.

Boon also touched on the rivalry between Mortal Kombat and Street Fighter, which began in the playgrounds of the early '90s and was recently thrust back into the headlines when new movies based on both franchises began their marketing push. Andrew Shultz, who plays Dan Hibiki in the upcoming Street Fighter film, delivered a fresh jab at the 2025 Game Awards, where the comedian claimed that the Mortal Kombat team “only care about money,” while excusing their absence from attending the award show to promote their film.

Speaking to Collider, Boon called the rivalry "fun," and insisted there are "no hard feelings." "At the end of the day, I think we'll see, when both films have been released, how they both did and stuff. But honestly, I'd love to see them both do well, just because there's an audience for more, which would be great," he said.

And on the subject of Marvel, NetherRealm is owned by Warner Bros. Games, which owns DC (NetherRealm's next game is rumored to be DC fighting game Injustice 3). It seems more likely that a DC guest star will pop into Mortal Kombat, rather than a rival character owned by Marvel. Of course, NetherRealm released Mortal Kombat vs. DC Universe in 2008, so it knows what it means to have Sub-Zero fight Batman.

Cade Onder is a freelancer for IGN's news team. He covers all things entertainment, including gaming, film, and more. You can find him on Twitter @Cade_Onder.

item.title: Google DeepMind partners with EVE Online for AI model testing
item.content:encoded:
Google’s AI-focused DeepMind division has taken a minority stake in the developer of popular sci-fi simulation EVE Online, saying it will use the game to study “intelligence in complex, dynamic, player-driven systems.”

The research partnership comes as the management behind EVE Online developer CCP Games announced that they have spent $120 million to buy themselves out from their former owners at South Korean publisher Pearl Abyss (Crimson Desert). The newly independent entity is being rebranded as Fenris Creations, which will continue to operate as normal without any restructuring or layoffs, the company said.

“Something that already behaves like a living world”

In today’s announcement, Fenris and DeepMind said that EVE Online presents “a uniquely rich environment for study,” especially when it comes to developing AI systems that use “long-horizon planning, memory, and continual learning.” DeepMind says it will conduct controlled experiments on its models in a specially designed offline version of the game running on a local server, without directly impacting the experience for online players. The two companies “will also explore new gameplay experiences enabled by these technologies,” they wrote.

Google DeepMind has a long history of using games as a proving ground for machine learning models, from enabling breakthroughs in complex board games like Go to outperforming humans in Atari VCS games and StarCraft, for example. More recently, the company has begun using so-called “virtual world” models to help AI systems learn to operate in physical reality.

Fenris CEO Hilmar Veigar Pétursson said in an open letter addressed to players that “EVE is one of the few environments where questions about intelligence can be explored inside something that already behaves like a living world.” Studying EVE will allow Google DeepMind’s models to explore “difficult problems, long timelines [and] strange possibilities,” he added.

“As a gamer and games producer, I’ve long admired EVE,” Google DeepMind Director Alexandre Moufarek said in a statement. “What the EVE community has created together with [Pétursson] and team is truly unparalleled in gaming. It is a one-of-a-kind simulation for testing general-purpose artificial intelligence in a safe sandbox environment. I’m excited to partner with the team at Fenris Creations to push the frontier of artificial intelligence and explore new player experiences.”



https://arstechnica.com/gaming/2026/05/google-deepmind-partners-with-eve-online-for-ai-model-testing/

item.title: Mina The Hollower Finally Launches Later This Month
item.content:encoded:
Shovel Knight developer Yacht Club Games has finally revealed a new release date for its long-awaited retro-inspired action game, Mina the Hollower. Thankfully, it’s only a few weeks away, as the game arrives on May 29th.

First announced in February 2022, Mina the Hollower draws inspiration from top-down Zelda, Castlevania, and sports an 8-bit presentation evoking Game Boy Color titles. It stars Mina, a famous scientist or “Hollower” who travels to a cursed island to help restore its lost power. Playing off her title, Mina can burrow underground, which is used in combat, puzzle-solving, and exploring the overworld and dungeons. Mina also wields a whip to attack enemies and can also use a variety of sidearms. Check out the most recent trailer from last year below to see the game in action (just disregard the out-of-date October release date). 

Mina the Hollower was originally crowdfunded on Kickstarter, and backers were the first to learn of the game’s new release date. The game was first scheduled to release last Halloween before it was delayed.

Mina the Hollower will launch on May 29 for PlayStation 5, Xbox Series X/S, Switch 2, Switch, and PC. For more on the game, check out this 2025 deep-dive feature where we spoke with Yacht Club Games founder Sean Velasco to learn new gameplay details. You should also check out our 2022 documentary about the creation of Mina the Hollower.

item.title: Speculation Grows About WoW Classic's Rumored Next Phase
item.content:encoded:
Could Blizzard be planning a new way to play World of Warcraft Classic? New rumors and visits by creators are fueling speculation that we might be looking at a WoW Classic+.

The report comes from Icy Veins and mentions that a number of creators, including Xaryu, have quietly been invited to Blizzard for something. There’s nothing that any of the invited can say about why they’re there, but speculation is ongoing that this might be a Classic+ in the works and ready for some input.

This also involves the launch of Titan Reforged in China, with servers that offer a version of WoW that takes three expansions: Vanilla, The Burning Crusade, and Wrath of the Lich King, and combines them. The combined build offers account-wide progression and plays out like level 80 endgame content, featuring raids scaled to 25-M. 

There was an unconfirmed leak last October that pointed to a Classic+ experience. Part of this unverified info made reference to WoW Classic ending with Mists of Pandaria Classic, Anniversary Edition of The Burning Crusade moving onto Wrath, and more changes. 

Could we see a form of Titan Reforged tailored to a Western release? We’ll have to wait and see, but the creator visits, especially by Classic-focused ones, are stoking the speculation.

With BlizzCon back this fall, of course Blizzard is preparing the big announcements we’ve already been promised, so we have to wait to see just how much of this speculation pans out.



https://www.mmorpg.com/news/speculation-grows-about-wow-classics-rumored-next-phase-2000137992
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Mina The Hollower Finally Lands On Switch 1 & 2 In Just A Few Weeks
item.content:encoded:
Squeaking into that Spring window

Yacht Club Games' highly anticipated Zelda-inspired adventure, Mina the Hollower, will be coming to Switch 1 and Switch 2 on 29th May 2026.

Not only that, the developer has also confirmed the game will cost $19.99 (other regional prices to be confirmed). The announcement came via a YouTube short on the official Yacht Club Games channel.

That's fantastic news, because we've been waiting a long time to see what Yacht Club can do with the Zelda formula and, from the limited-time demo last year and the various gameplay videos and impressions we've read, this could be something special.

There's not much else to add here! The Switch 2 version supports 120fps, but there are few other differences, really.

Many have been patiently waiting for the spooky adventure, which was originally due to release on 31st October 2025, before Yacht Club delayed it just a few weeks before launch. But the game went gold just a few weeks ago, prompting speculation that a release date was imminent. And look at that, here we are in early May with the news.

Are you excited for Mina the Hollower? Will you be grabbing this on Switch 1 or Switch 2? Squeak in the comments and let us know.

[source youtube.com]

Alana has been with Nintendo Life since 2022, and while RPGs are her first love, Nintendo is a close second. She enjoys nothing more than overthinking battle strategies, characters, and stories. She also wishes she was a Sega air pirate.

  • Author Profile
  • Bluesky
  • Reply

item.title: Nintendo Stock Hits Longest Losing Streak Since 2016 as Investors Demand Switch 2 Price Hike Despite Pokopia & Mario Galaxy
item.content:encoded:
Today, Bloomberg's Tech In Depth newsletter covers a thorny subject: should Nintendo raise the Switch 2 pricing in response to the current economic environment and the ongoing memory shortage?

Sony did, across its entire PlayStation ecosystem (PS5, PS5 Slim, PS5 Pro, and PlayStation Portal). Nintendo, on the other hand, has maintained steadfast so far, but Bloomberg points out that investors aren't quite happy with this strategy. The company's stock has been on a steady decline for five consecutive months, marking the longest sustained decline in Nintendo's stock since 2016. This is despite the Nintendo Switch 2 continuing to sell extremely well, not to mention the success of a game like Pokémon Pokopia (2.2 million units sold in the first four days) and of a film like The Super Mario Galaxy Movie, which has topped the global box office for 2026 and is nearing 900 million gross.

So, what's the issue? Well, Bloomberg says investors are "deeply concerned" that the Nintendo Switch 2 is not very profitable at its current $450 price. On Friday, during the company's latest quarterly earnings call, Nintendo president Shuntaro Furukawa is expected to address the pricing issue once again.

Bloomberg asked a few key analysts for their take on the company's strategy, and opinions are mixed. Toyo Research Advice analyst Hideki Yasuda believes Nintendo's stock will continue to go down until the console's price gets a hike:

The market is punishing stocks that lack inflation-hedging power.

Wedbush Securities analyst Michael Pachter, on the other hand, reckons it would be foolish of them to raise the price now.

I think they would be foolish to raise prices. The consumer is hurting — people are paying more for gasoline and food, and when prices go up, entertainment budgets are one of the first things to go.

Pelham Smithers Associates, an independent research provider covering the Japanese stock market, shared a note saying that Nintendo simply cannot afford to make a mistake either way.

If they make a mistake on pricing, whichever way it goes, we run into the Wii U problem — the situation will just get worse and worse.

Given Nintendo's historical caution, I wouldn't rule out a wait-and-see approach, possibly deferring the decision to a later date. We'll know in a couple of days if that's their choice.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/nintendo-stock-longest-losing-streak-2016-investors-switch-2-price-hike/

item.title: Review: TetherGeist (Switch) - Satisfying Platforming That Owes A Debt To Celeste
item.content:encoded:
2D platformers are easy to find. They’re one of the oldest genres in video games, and thanks to that long pedigree, they’ve been getting made for nearly the entire industry’s history. It would be impossible to play through them all in your lifetime.

Great 2D platformers, though, are rare. TetherGeist belongs in that rare category, and it may be my favourite 2D platformer I’ve played since Celeste. It follows in the footsteps of the very best, and yet, it blazes its own trail with something new. Smart level design and progression keep things constantly fresh, and collectibles galore offer extra challenges and reward exploration.

You play as Mae, a young girl from a village of shamans. Every year, a group from the village takes part in a ritual where they leave their physical bodies and take a “spirit flight” to the Fount of Souls. It’s a big moment in every shaman’s life, with everyone in Mae’s village talking about how it changed their lives.

Mae, however, is not like the other shamans. She’s tethered, meaning she can only leave her physical body for a short time and short distance. So, her journey looks a little different than the other girls in the village. Luckily, Mae’s the one starring in a precision platformer.

The tether is the crux of what makes the game so special. At first, I found myself wondering how the game could sustain itself on this single mechanic; I was so happy to be proven wrong. As you progress, you’ll find new types of tethers, each with their own properties. One gives you the ability to shoot an orb and later teleport to it. Another gives you a speed boost after you use it, à la Metroid’s Shinespark, allowing you to quickly run across hot magma without burning.

Each level contains the basic tether and the area’s newly introduced variant, letting you get the basics down while introducing new mechanics that interact with each one in unique ways. These different tethers make some areas feel like puzzles, forcing you to think about how to use them, what order to collect them in, and when to use your more basic platforming moveset like wall jumping to save your resources. There’s an 'intended' solution for each screen, sure. But it’s also a glorious feeling to get to the end of a screen with resources left over and feel like you pulled one over on the game.

Mae’s tether and the precision platformer genre are natural pairs, and the way that the game works pairs well with the game’s theme of disability. Mae’s journey is intentionally very different, and it is difficult. But respawns are instant. For every setback (I had 1,664 “setbacks,” to be exact), Mae always gets right back up, ready to try again.

Beyond getting to the end of each level, there’s more to find. Specifically, Atropa Blossoms. These collectibles can be hidden behind foliage or in secret rooms, or require you to pull off a challenging manoeuvre to collect them close to the main path. I was never bored trying to find them.

It’s easy, too, to hunt down those last collectibles for your checklist. Stages are split into different sections, and those sections have their own tracker for how many blossoms there are. It was handy to go back and try to hunt down stray blossoms, though I’m still far from 100%.

Between standard levels, you’ll also stop in various towns on your pilgrimage. These towns are well worth exploring, not just because they contain a few Atropa Blossoms, found by exploring or helping out citizens in town, but because the people in them are well worth meeting.

Take Monty in Shellwood, for instance. Every year in this town, there’s a ceremony where they burn a traditional worm effigy. Monty, the town crier, has always lit the effigy. But, in recent years, Monty has been losing his eyesight. This boiled over at the previous ceremony, where he burned a house down instead of lighting the effigy. One villager suggests the solution is to “sneak up behind him and conk him over the head.” Mae decides to go talk to him instead.

This interaction defines the writing in Tethergeist at its best. When Mae finally does have the conversation with Monty, she suggests that Monty transitions to be “torch bearer emeritus,” a way for him to be present, but pass the torch to someone else – literally and figuratively. It’s still an absurd situation, but there’s also an undeniable heart at its centre.

It’s unfortunate, then, that Tethergeist’s main narrative is its Achilles heel. It’s delivered well enough, mostly told through short dialogue exchanges you have with various characters during levels. But the problem is, as fun as Mae is, and as much as she helps other people, she goes through very little growth of her own, leaving her as more of a caricature than a character.

Presentation-wise, Tethergeist isn’t especially memorable, but it gets the job done. The pixel art looks great in action, and I really like the character portraits in main story dialogue exchanges. The music isn’t memorable, either, but the sound effects when using your abilities are quite satisfying. The stylish art looks great on handheld and in docked mode, and there are no performance issues to speak of.

One issue that came up was some visual bugs in the form of screen tearing, where the entire screen was taken over by lines of colours. It was easy enough to wait around and let it pass within a few seconds, but it was a bit distracting.

It’s obvious that developer O. and Co. was majorly influenced by Celeste, but in most ways, it rises to the occasion. Its gameplay has just as many varied mechanics, if not more. It’s built for speedrunners (there’s an optional in-game timer), and begging to be broken open. It lacks in some of those presentation areas Maddy Thorson's classic thrived in — music and story in particular — but if you like Celeste for its gameplay, you’d be hard-pressed to find a better 'successor' this year.

Conclusion

Tethergeist is a great precision platformer. A flat narrative and some small visual bugs somewhat muddy the waters, but it’s filled with fresh ideas. The tether mechanic at its core tests your reflexes and brains in equal measure, and I found myself always on the hunt for the Atropa Blossoms. Between those levels, I found a lot of fun in interacting with villagers across the world.

Perfectly paced, no one mechanic overstays its welcome, and it felt like it ended at the perfect time, around 10 hours in length. I’m not a completionist, but I can definitely see myself picking away at some collectibles. And if they ever drop a challenge level pack, testing my knowledge of all the tethers? I’ll be there day one.



https://www.nintendolife.com/reviews/switch-eshop/tethergeist
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: TetherGeist Review – Celeste on a Leash
item.content:encoded:
If you've been in the mood for a narrative-focused precision platformer, TetherGeist is the latest one to test your skills. The game takes place in a world where shamans can astrally project their spirits wherever they want and teleport their bodies to that place. It's a ubiquitous mode of transportation, and kids go through a rite of passage called the Binding to certify their abilities, much like driving.

This sounds like the total opposite of what you'd want from a precision platformer, right? However, Mae, the protagonist, isn't like the others. Her spirit is tethered to her, drastically limiting her ability to project compared to her friends. Because of her limitations, she's denied from participating in the Binding—but that's not stopping her from trying and journeying to the Fount of Souls.

Narratively, this limitation explores what it's like to live with a disability as Mae interacts with the various NPCs in the world. From a gameplay perspective, it creates challenging precision platforming puzzles that test your reaction time and lateral thinking. TetherGeist doesn't pull its punches, though, making it a challenging yet rewarding experience.

TetherGeist – Platforming in the Spirit of Celeste

TetherGeist clearly takes notes from Celeste in the game's structure. Each bite-sized level is punctuated with checkpoints, and you navigate to the other end of the screen by jumping and projecting over, around, and through obstacles. It's generally difficult, with some rooms feeling almost insurmountable at first.

To astrally project, Mae has to pick up items called Azae. There are seven different kinds, each giving her a different ability. For example, the first one lets you simply move her tethered spirit a short distance and teleport to it. Another launches her toward the spirit, allowing her to break obstacles and bounce off walls.

These different powers are introduced slowly throughout TetherGeist, and I found it to be a good pace for learning each one without feeling overwhelmed. Generally, each power is introduced in its most basic state, as described above, but then you run into obstacles or tools that modify how they work.

My favorite example is the Azae that lets Mae project her spirit as a ball in a straight line. It banks off walls and goes through spiky thorns, encouraging you to think geometrically. Then you run into red areas that will dissolve your spirit, so you have to avoid those. And then there are colored portals that transport your spirit, so you have to think with portals. Oh, and there are switches that can only be toggled with your spirit, too.

That single Azae is so mechanically dense, and only a few others match its versatility. Overall, these powers create a clever movement system in TetherGeist that feels like a puzzle; each maneuver needs to be perfectly executed to get to the other side.

That's where the generous checkpoint system comes in handy. You're probably going to die a lot. There's an achievement for failing 1,000 times, for context. Some levels can get a little frustrating, partially because despite all the tools at your disposal, it usually feels like there's only one way to succeed.

The Azae are placed in a way that directs you along an intended path, and because the tools are so specific, there isn't much room for improvising. The spirit ball Azae I've found gives the most flexibility, but even then, it's a narrow band of freedom. I'm no speedrunner, though, so your mileage may vary if you have that sort of experience.

Perhaps the trickiest power in my experience is one that bounces you toward or away from floating mushrooms. The other powers slow down the game, giving you time to dial in your movements. The mushroom power, however, is a one-and-done motion. You're either at the perfect angle or you're not, based on your reaction time.

Sometimes, it gets frustrating to miss a specific movement and have to redo the entire movement sequence over and over. There's a precision in TetherGeist that allows for very little leeway—you have to be almost perfect, or you won't make it. Unfortunately, sometimes the camera doesn't quite pan fast enough to keep up with the action, too.

This level of precision is doubly true for the red atropa flowers, which are optional extra collectibles. There are over 200 in the game, and a certain number of them are tied to achieving the true ending. I didn't get enough for it, but I also feel satisfied with my experience as is.

Regardless of the moments of frustration, there's a simple joy in TetherGeist. The easy levels let you appreciate the clever game design, while the more difficult levels reward you with the sheer, blissful satisfaction of finally conquering that mountain.

A Coming-of-Age Story About Coming to Terms With Yourself

The game's systems complement the narrative, giving agency to Mae's limitations as someone with a tethered spirit. Some people in the various hub worlds pity her for having a tether, and others even recommend finding a "cure" before embarking on this journey. After all, everyone else can move their spirits freely; Mae would simply be doing it the hard way.

However, Mae still perseveres. She doesn't want to be held back by her tether—in fact, she isn't really held back by anything. It's just that she has to do things differently. Some other characters even compliment her resourcefulness and skill, reiterating that she simply has to do things differently.

One of TetherGeist's strengths comes from the writing. It's insightful and charming, teeing up meaningful messages between playful, silly moments. There are giant forest frogs, pineapple-obsessed aliens, and sauna-locked ancient spirits to make you chuckle or smirk, all while your mind ponders why people choose to pity Mae when she's clearly capable all on her own.

Within this challenging precision platformer, there are many lessons about understanding people with disabilities. When given the appropriate tools and opportunities, they're just as capable as anyone else. Sure, the game can be difficult and frustrating—but you can still complete it, even if it's the "hard way" compared to Mae's peers. In that way, the gameplay is a nice metaphor that reflects the story.

There's a foil character in TetherGeist named Foyal (coincidence? Definitely not.) who shows a different take on having disabilities. She has a fear-based motivation to "cure" herself, and it's interesting seeing how her story develops alongside Mae, who chooses to accept herself instead.

By the time I got to the end, I found myself really thinking about and connecting with the messages in the story. It treats people with disabilities with respect and encourages the player to think more about what it means to live with one—either for themself or for others.

TetherGeist Review | Final Thoughts

There's a part where Mae says something along the lines of, "Who cares if I did it the wrong way? At least I did it!" In a world where so many people tell Mae that she's incapable of doing things the same way as her peers, it's a powerful expression of her self-confidence.

That message encapsulates all the great narrative beats in TetherGeist. Even in its hardest, most gut-wrenching moments, there's an aura of perseverance in the face of seemingly insurmountable difficulty.

And that's reflected in the gameplay as well. The game itself can be tough and demanding, but moving around the world the "wrong way" feels fun and exciting. It's a novel take on platformers, and every hazard and obstacle feels fine-tuned specifically for this game.

Unfortunately, after some time with the game, the novelty wears away and reveals the rails that keep you in a lane. It's still a fun ride—albeit an occasionally frustrating one—that's worth experiencing, especially if you're itching for a challenge in the vein of Celeste.

TetherGeist was reviewed on a Steam Deck with a copy provided by the developer over 9 hours of gameplay; all screenshots were taken during the review process. 

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/reviews/tethergeist-review-celeste-on-leash

item.title: EA Sets A New $8 Billion Sales Record Just Months After Laying Off Battlefield 6 Developers
item.content:encoded:
Electronic Arts just wrapped its last year as a publicly traded company with a new sales record. Control 2 is on track and not over-budget. The latest milestone on Microsoft’s “return to Xbox” road map is a new boot logo. It’s your Kotaku Checkpoint roundup for May 6, 2026, where we are getting to know a new member of the Xbox team who has a great Guitar Hero analogy for AI.

GameStop’s cosmic-brain CEO Ryan Cohen recently went on the OpenAI-owned TBPN network to do damage control on his eBay takeover bid after going on CNBC and sounding like he’d just suffered a concussion. The stock has still not recovered from its post-cable-news slump. GameStop employees are not impressed.

EA had a great year and it’s all thanks to Battlefield 6

The best-selling game of 2025 propelled the soon-to-be-Saudi-Arabia-owned publisher to a new annual sales record of $8.026 billion. While its quarterly earnings were marked by a year-over-year decline in live-service revenue, investors are still set to receive a nice dividend ahead of their private equity buyout this summer.

“Driven by our talented teams and disciplined execution, we delivered a record FY26, highlighted by the incredibly successful launch of our iconic Battlefield franchise,” CEO Andrew Wilson said in a press release. “With the recent completion of a debt process that was met with strong investor demand and our ongoing constructive engagement with regulators, we look ahead to closing the transaction and the opportunities it will unlock.”

The earnings report comes just two months after an unconfirmed number of Battlefield 6 developers were laid off across the company despite the game’s blockbuster success. “We’ve made select changes within our Battlefield organization to better align our teams around what matters most to our community,” EA said in a statement at the time. “Battlefield remains one of our biggest priorities, and we’re continuing to invest in the franchise, guided by player feedback and insights from Battlefield Labs.”

There’s a chance we could get Final Fantasy 7 Remake part 3 DLC

Part 1 got Intergrade, part 2 got nothing. What about part 3? That depends on “how strongly fans continue to support Final Fantasy 7 as both a title and a franchise,” Naoki Hamaguchi said in a new interview with ntower (via Gamesradar). He remains “open to exploring those possibilities proactively.”

Control 2 only cost an estimated $50 million and Remedy wants to keep it that way

“The team and the studio has done excellent work to stay on track. Has done excellent work to build a triple-A game on a relatively small budget. That’s something we’ve seen from Remedy before. That’s something we’ll see again from Remedy,” new CEO Jean-Charles Gaudechon said in a recent earning’s call (via Game Developer).

He continued, “Honestly, there’s something pretty incredible about the way games are being built at Remedy and are being thought through and managed. It has not always been the case, I know that. There have been some hiccups in the past. But the team has done incredible work on Control Resonant.”

Xbox has a new boot-up logo

New boot up coming next Wednesday. Sound on! pic.twitter.com/9HHrZHwjpH

— Asha (@asha_shar) May 6, 2026

Joke’s on them: I never turn my Series S off.

Full-priced game sales have become a luxury

62 percent of player no longer buy full-price games, according to a new survey of gaming audiences by IGN Entertainment. “[Gen X] come from a point of view of midnight opening, full price games, console base… [they] want to get everything they possibly can, get as much juice out of that lemon as possible,” company SVP of marketing Karl Stewart said (via GamesIndustry.biz).

He continued, “Whereas you look at Gen Z, who live in a world of platforms… games don’t end, but social and community becomes a massive part of their DNA. So they want to be a part of a community where they’re able to say, ‘I’m the most informed. I know most about this game.'”

Investors might be pressuring Nintendo to raise the price of the Switch 2

The company’s stock price has fallen five months in a row as it holds the line on a $450 console even as Sony and Microsoft have raised prices on their respective machines. Bloomberg reports that investors want to see a similar move from the Mario maker in order to “protect” profit margins on the Switch 2. Of course, Nintendo also has to make sure it sells a lot of the new console, and so far there have been plenty of reasons for players to sit tight on the last-gen version.

Dying Light franchise director leaves after 13 years

Tymon Smektala joined Techland in 2013 as a game designer and recently served as head of the entire zombie franchise. He wrote on LinkedIn, “After years with Dying Light, I’m moving into a new chapter. It’s been an incredible journey, and I’m proud of what we’ve built.”

ICYMI:

  • How does Tomodachi Life: Living The Dream measure up to the 3DS game? Read our review
  • Hands-on impressions of Lego Batman reveal a clever mashup of gritty sandbox and absurd slapstick
  • Saros was supposed to be the easier version of Returnal but it’s still pretty damn hard

Watch this:



https://kotaku.com/ea-sets-a-new-8-billion-sales-record-just-months-after-laying-off-battlefield-6-developers-2000693589

item.title: New Xbox Boot Up Sound Revealed By CEO
item.content:encoded:
Xbox CEO has revealed a new boot-up sound for consoles, which should arrive next week.

Since the arrival of the new CEO, we have seen all kinds of changes at Xbox, including reducing the price of Game Pass, revealing new tiers, and much more. The latest change has been made to match the brand-new Xbox logo; however, the new sound feels awfully similar to the previous iteration.

New Xbox Boot-Up Sound Makes Small Tweaks Over the Previous One

Interested readers can check out the new Xbox boot-up sound in the CEO Asha Sharma’s latest post on X (formerly Twitter). Sharma also stated that the new sound will arrive next Wednesday, May 13. Though the latest version and the previous iteration of the sound are very close, there are some audible tweaks. The sound feels higher-pitched, is shorter in duration, and matches the animation they have set for the new logo pretty well. Interested readers can check out the comparison video below:

Since Asha Sharma’s arrival, players have been seeing more frequent changes towards Xbox. Just recently, the CEO announced that Microsoft will no longer develop Copilot for Xbox and is winding down mobile development as well. Along with that, the Xbox leadership is seeing further changes: more former Microsoft CoreAI folks have joined the company’s leadership team.

In other news, Xbox Game Pass brings a stacked lineup for May 2026 Wave 1 with Forza Horizon 6 headlining the collection. Here are more details. Microsoft has announced a new Game Dev Update show that will give an update on Xbox’s Project Helix next-gen console. What are your thoughts on the new Xbox boot-up sound? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about Final Fantasy 7 Remake Part 3 director being open to exploring DLC possibilities. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

Real-time news, exclusive podcasts, push alerts, and a better reading experience.
